It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Assembling an experimentalist regime: Transnational governance interactions in the forest sector Christine Overdevest University of Florida, Gainesville, FL, USA Jonathan Zeitlin University of Amsterdam, Amsterdam, the Netherlands Abstract Transnational governance initiatives increasingly face the problem of regime complexity in which a proliferation of regulatory schemes operate in the same policy domain, supported by varying combinations of public and private actors. The literature suggests that such regime complexity can lead to forum shopping and other self-interested strategies, which undermine the effectiveness of transnational regulation. Based on the design principles of experimentalist governance, this paper identiﬁes a variety of pathways and mechanisms, which promote productive interactions in regime complexes. We use the case of the EU’s Forest Law Enforcement Governance and Trade (FLEGT) initiative, interacting with private certiﬁcation schemes and public legal timber regulations, includ- ing those of third countries such as the US and China, to demonstrate how an increasingly comprehensive transnational regime can be assembled by linking together distinct components of a regime complex.We argue that it is the experimentalist features of this initiative and its regulatory interactions, which accommodate local diversity and foster recursive learning from decentralized implementation experience, that make it possible to build up a ﬂexible and adaptive transnational governance regime from an assemblage of interconnected pieces, even in situations where interests diverge and no hegemon can impose its own will. Keywords: European Union, experimentalism, FLEGT, forestry, regime complexity, transnational governance. 1. Introduction It is a commonplace of international relations theory that effective, integrated regulatory regimes cannot easily be constructed in issue areas characterized by divergent interests and beliefs among key actors, where there is no hegemon with the power to impose a single set of rules (Hasenclever et al. 2000; Keohane & Victor 2011). The result in such conditions is typically regime complexity: a proliferation of regulatory schemes operating in the same policy domain, supported by varying combinations of public and private actors, including states, international organizations, businesses, and NGOs. Recently, however, some scholars working in this area have identiﬁed the possibility of productive interactions emerging, or being deliberately orchestrated, among the compo- nents of such transnational regime complexes (Alter & Meunier 2009; Abbott & Snidal 2009b, 2010; Keohane & Victor 2011). This special issue is itself a reﬂection of such rethinking, as the editorial introduction proposes a new conceptual framework for ana- lyzing both positive and negative interactions between transnational business governance initiatives operating in the same economic sector or policy domain (Eberlein et al. 2012). In this paper, we build on such rethinking to outline a theoretically informed route to the stepwise construction of a joined-up transnational governance regime in hotly con- tested policy ﬁelds where no single actor can enforce a unilateral solution. We use the case of the European Union’s Forest Law Enforcement Governance and Trade (FLEGT) ini- tiative, interacting with private certiﬁcation schemes and public legal timber regulations, including those of third countries such as the US and China, to illustrate how an increas- ingly comprehensive transnational regime can be assembled de facto, if not de jure, by linking together distinct components of a regime complex. We highlight the experimen- talist features of the FLEGT initiative and its regulatory interactions, arguing that it is precisely these features, which accommodate local diversity and promote recursive learn- ing from decentralized implementation experience, that make it possible to build up a ﬂexible and adaptive transnational governance regime from an assemblage of intercon- nected pieces. 2. Complexity and experimentalism in transnational regime formation 2.1. Regime complexity Regime complexity may be deﬁned as a situation in which there is no single, uniﬁed body of hierarchically imposed rules governing a transnational issue area or policy domain, but instead a set of parallel or overlapping regulatory institutions. In a recent survey, Alter and Meunier (2009) sketch out the possible consequences of such plural institutional arrangements for transnational governance. Like most previous commen- tators, they argue that regime complexity increases the likelihood of “cross-institutional political strategies,” such as forum shopping, regime shifting, and strategic inconsis- tency. Faced with competing institutions and rules, actors will exploit regulatory diver- sity to pursue self-interested goals and particularistic advantages. In forum shopping, actors strategically select from among a set of institutional venues in hopes of obtaining a decision that will advance their own speciﬁc interests. In regime shifting, they try to move the regulatory agenda for a particular issue from one institution to another in order to reshape the global set of rules. In strategic inconsistency, actors intentionally create a contradictory rule or exploit contradictions between overlapping institutions in order to weaken the effect of existing disadvantageous rules. Alter and Meunier argue that regime complexity creates greater structural opportunities for these cross- institutional strategies and suggest further research to evaluate their frequency and impact. Although Alter and Meunier (2009) focus primarily on the negative consequences of regime complexity, they also suggest that it may generate more positive interactions Assembling an experimentalist regime C. Overdevest and J. Zeitlin © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 2 Page 3: between parallel or overlapping institutions. Thus competition between regimes can promote productive experimentation by actors pursuing different approaches, reduce the risk of failure of any single institution, stimulate cross-fertilization and horizontal learn- ing, and enhance accountability by creating new opportunities for dissatisﬁed parties to challenge existing rules. But Alter and Meunier do not specify under what conditions such competition can produce these positive effects, nor do they identify institutional strategies for promoting them. Keohane and Victor (2011) elaborate further on the potential for regime complexity to generate positive interactions in transnational governance. In their view, “loosely coupled” regime complexes, or sets of interlinked institutions without an overall archi- tecture, often emerge as a creative response to the failure of attempts to create a more comprehensive and integrated international regime.Where the interests and beliefs of key actors persistently diverge and there is no hegemonic power, a weak or non-existent international regime is the most likely result. Under these conditions, groups of actors may create narrower institutions in order to move parts of the regulatory ﬁeld forward. These uncoordinated moves may actually produce a stronger de facto regulatory regime. For instance, regime fragmentation increases the probability that individual components of complex problems such as climate change can be tackled separately and solutions adapted to local or regional conditions and concerns. In this way, if the ﬁrst-best world of a coherent, broadly agreed global regulatory regime proves politically unfeasible, there may be a second-best world in which a loosely coupled regime complex improves regu- latory outcomes in comparison with the real alternative of a weak or non-existent over- arching regime. In a more radical departure from standard regime theory, Keohane and Victor go on to argue that such loosely coupled regime complexes may also be more ﬂexible across issues and adaptable over time than a hierarchical system of rules imposed by a monopolistic international institution. Rather than representing a second-best alterna- tive to a broadly agreed global regulatory regime, regime complexes may thus offer a superior starting point for building a joined-up, sustainable set of transnational gov- ernance institutions. Keohane and Victor set out a number of criteria for evaluating actually existing regime complexes in terms of their coherence, accountability, determinacy, sustainability, epistemic quality, and fairness. But they do not provide a road map for the emergence of regime complexes with these beneﬁcial features, nor do they identify a governance architecture within them for learning from local experimentation. Recent work on transnational private, public–private, and multi-stakeholder regu- lation reaches similar conclusions. Abbott and Snidal (2009a,b) observe that the pro- liferation of Regulatory Standard Setting (RSS) schemes in ﬁelds like labor rights, human rights, and the environment, can undermine the effectiveness of transnational governance by raising compliance costs for ﬁrms, creating opportunities for both ﬁrms and states to shop around for the weakest or most favorable standards, and confusing consumers and other public audiences. But they also argue that the multiplicity of competing RSS schemes has a number of salient virtues in comparison to “Interna- tional Old Governance” (hierarchical regimes led by intergovernmental organizations or IGOs): facilitating adaptation of standards and procedures to local circumstances; promoting regulatory experimentation; and avoiding institutional capture, by obliging RSS schemes to compete with one another for legitimacy and public support (Abbott Assembling an experimentalist regime C. Overdevest and J. Zeitlin © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 3 Page 4: & Snidal 2009b). To retain the beneﬁts of multiplicity within “Transnational New Gov- ernance” while minimizing the disadvantages of complexity, Abbott and Snidal recom- mend that states and IGOs should “orchestrate” RSSs by establishing substantive and procedural criteria for approved schemes, and publicizing the results to consumers and other audiences; providing material beneﬁts to ﬁrms meeting the standards of approved schemes such as relaxed administrative requirements or preferential access to loans, grants, and contracts; and fostering collaboration and comparison among com- peting schemes to identify, diffuse, and scale-up effective practices and approaches (Abbott & Snidal 2009b, 2010). Like Keohane and Victor, however, Abbott and Snidal do not delineate a governance architecture within which local experimentation and dispersed expertise can be systematically combined with coordinated learning and regime coherence. 2.2. Experimentalism Experimentalism, we argue, provides just such a governance architecture. Deﬁned in the most general terms, experimentalist governance is a recursive process of provisional goal setting and revision based on learning from comparison of alternative approaches to advancing these goals in different contexts. Experimentalist governance in its most developed form involves a multi-level architecture, whose four elements are linked in an iterative cycle. First, broad framework goals (such as “sustainable forests” or “legally harvested timber”) and metrics for gauging their achievement are provisionally established by some combination of “central” and “local” units, in consultation with relevant stakeholders. Second, local units are given broad discretion to pursue these goals in their own way. These “local” units can be public, private, or hybrid partner- ships. In regulatory systems, they will typically be private ﬁrms and the territorial authorities or branch organizations to which these immediately respond. But, third, as a condition of this autonomy, these units must report regularly on their performance and participate in a peer review in which their results are compared with those of others employing different means to the same ends. Where they are not making good progress against the agreed indicators, the local units are expected to show that they are taking appropriate corrective measures, informed by the experience of their peers. Fourth and ﬁnally, the goals, metrics, and decision-making procedures themselves are periodically revised by a widening circle of actors in response to the problems and possibilities revealed by the review process, and the cycle repeats (Sabel & Zeitlin 2012). These four key elements should be understood as a set of necessary functions, which can be performed through a variety of possible institutional arrangements. There is in such an experimentalist architecture no one-to-one mapping of governance functions to speciﬁc institutional mechanisms or policy instruments, and vice versa.A single function, such as monitoring and review of implementation experience, can be performed through a variety of institutional devices, operating singly or in combination. Conversely, a single institutional mechanism, such as a formal peer review, can perform a number of distinct governance functions, such as assessing the comparative effectiveness of different imple- mentation approaches, holding local units accountable for their relative performance, identifying areas where new forms of national or transnational capacity building are required, and contributing to the redeﬁnition of common policy objectives (Sabel & Zeitlin 2008). Zeitlin © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 4 Page 5: Experimentalist governance architectures of this type have become pervasively insti- tutionalized across the European Union, covering a broad array of policy domains from regulation of energy, telecommunications, ﬁnancial services, and competition through food and drug safety, data privacy, and environmental protection, to justice, security, and anti-discrimination rights (Sabel & Zeitlin 2008, 2010). But experimentalist governance architectures with similar properties can also be widely found in the United States and other developed democracies, both in the regulation of public health and safety risks, such as nuclear power, food processing, and environmental pollution, and in the provi- sion of public services like education and child welfare (Sabel & Simon 2011). Trans- national experimentalist regimes likewise appear to be emerging across a number of major issue areas, such as disability rights, data privacy, food safety, and environmental sustainability (Sabel & Zeitlin 2011). Experimentalist governance architectures have a number of salient virtues. First, they accommodate diversity in adapting general goals to varied local contexts, rather than imposing uniform, one-size-ﬁts-all solutions. Second, they provide a mechanism for coordinated learning from local experimentation through disciplined comparison of different approaches to advancing broad common goals. Third, both the goals themselves and the means for achieving them are explicitly conceived as provisional and subject to revision in the light of experience, so that problems identiﬁed in one phase of imple- mentation can be corrected in the next. For each of these reasons, such governance architectures have emerged as a widespread response to turbulent, polyarchic environ- ments, where strategic uncertainty means that effective solutions to problems can only be determined in the course of pursuing them, while a multi-polar distribution of power means that no single actor can impose her own preferred solution without taking into account the views of others. The scope conditions for experimentalist governance are thus precisely the opposite of those for regime formation in standard international relations theory. For the latter, as we have seen, the formation of a comprehensive international regime depends on a convergence of interests and beliefs among the key actors, or the capacity of a hegemonic power to impose her preferred rules. Experimentalist governance, by contrast, depends on strategic uncertainty, a situation in which actors do not know their precise goals or how best to achieve them ex ante, but must discover both in the course of problem- solving, as well as on a polyarchic or multi-polar distribution of power, where no single actor can enforce a unilateral solution. Thus under conditions of polyarchy and disagree- ment among the parties, where standard international relations theory sees bleak pros- pects for creating a uniﬁed, effective multilateral regime, experimentalism discerns instead the possibility of building a new type of transnational regime with a different governance architecture. Because of their reﬂexive, self-revising capacity and deliberately corrigible design, such experimentalist governance architectures are also well-adapted to cope with volatile, rapidly changing environments characterized by deep uncertainty, which prominent theorists likeYoung (2006) and Keohane andVictor (2011) consider the critical contemporary challenge to sustaining effective international regimes. 2.3. Emergent pathways and causal mechanisms Experimentalist governance appears particularly well-suited to transnational domains, where there is no overarching sovereign with authority to set common goals, and where the diversity of local conditions and practices makes adoption and enforcement of Assembling an experimentalist regime C. Overdevest and J. Zeitlin © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 5 Page 6: uniform ﬁxed rules even less feasible than in domestic settings. Yet the very polyarchy and diversity that make experimentalist governance attractive under such conditions can also make it difﬁcult to get a transnational experimentalist regime off the ground. Thus, too many participants with sharply different perspectives may make it hard to reach an initial agreement on common framework goals. Conversely, a single powerful player may be able to veto other proposed solutions even if he cannot impose his own. In some cases, an experimentalist regime may nonetheless be created through the established multilateral procedures for negotiating international agreements, as a result of reﬂexive learning by state and non-state actors from the failures of more conventional approaches. The clearest example is the 2008 UN Convention on the Rights of Disabled Persons (CRDP). Traditional regimes of this kind contain catalogues of speciﬁc obliga- tions for states and sporadic international monitoring, understood as an analogue and (ideally) precursor to judicial enforcement. The CRDP, as de Búrca (2010) documents, arose out of a sustained debate among participating governments and NGOs about the deﬁciencies of such international human rights treaties. It departs from the model of formalist law strictly enforced by a court by incorporating many experimentalist features, including broad, open-ended goals such as“reasonable accommodation”for the disabled; participation of national NGOs and human rights institutions in implementation moni- toring; and annual review of its operations on the basis of comparative national data by an inclusive conference of stakeholders. Conversely, a transnational experimentalist governance architecture may also emerge through“cooperative decentralization”of an established international regime in response to failed attempts at imposing uniform universally applicable standards. Something of this kind may be occurring in the ﬁeld of ﬁnancial regulation, where pervasive differences in national and regional circumstances have led in the past to “sham compliance” with tightly harmonized global rules. Thus the new Financial Stability Board, as Helleiner and Pagliari (2011: p. 193) argue, appears to be moving ﬁtfully toward “the development and promotion of broad principles-based regulatory standards.” These would allow for a substantial margin of policy autonomy to accommodate regional and national diversity, supported by “activities such as information-sharing, research collaboration, early warning systems, and capacity building.” Compliance with these broad regulatory stan- dards would then be secured through a combination of regular peer reviews, periodic assessments by international ﬁnancial institutions, and restriction of market access for non-conforming jurisdictions. Often, however, the familiar coordination and collective action problems discussed earlier will block the initial formation of a comprehensive multilateral experimentalist regime. But that is only the beginning, not the end of the story. Because they are deﬁned in functional rather than structural terms, experimentalist governance architectures can take a variety of institutional forms. They can be built in multiple settings at different territorial scales, which can be nested within one another vertically and joined up hori- zontally. A number of emergent pathways and causal mechanisms can be identiﬁed through which transnational experimentalist regimes may be assembled piece by piece in this way, rather than being constructed as a uniﬁed whole through conventional multi- lateral procedures. These pathways should be understood analytically as stylized, ideal- typical trajectories leading from a characteristic starting point (national or international, public or private) toward the emergence of a transnational experimentalist regime, while Assembling an experimentalist regime C. Whether these pathways originate with public or private actors, or at the national or the international level, they converge on a multi-level, multi-actor governance architecture, which, in practice, should efface the relevance of these distinc- tions and thus the relevance of particular starting points and development patterns. In this sense, these experimentalist mechanisms can also be understood as devices for overcoming the path dependency and institutional inertia which many standard theorists consider endemic to transnational regimes, both public and private (Büthe & Mattli 2011; Keohane & Victor 2011). In our analysis of the emergence of a transnational experimentalist regime for sus- tainable forestry and the control of illegal logging, we will focus on four such ideal-typical pathways. The ﬁrst involves the creation of private experimentalist regimes in response to impasses in multilateral negotiations and inaction by public authorities, followed by their diffusion vertically along supply chains and horizontally within industry associations. In forestry, as we will see in the next section, a transnational coalition led by environmental NGOs established a private scheme to develop sustainable management standards and certify their application in response to the governance gap resulting from the failure of earlier intergovernmental efforts to agree to a binding global forestry convention. The Forest Stewardship Council (FSC), as we shall also see, has many experimentalist features, including not only its multi-stakeholder governance structure and deliberative decision- making procedures, but also its broad, principles-based standards, adapted to local conditions by national or regional chapters; continuous monitoring, independent veriﬁcation, and revision of individual forest management plans; and full traceability of certiﬁed wood from initial harvest to ﬁnal point of sale. One mechanism, through which private forest certiﬁcation has expanded and devel- oped, is vertical diffusion along supply chains from downstream customers to upstream producers. Thus retailers, branded manufacturers, and government procurement agen- cies have responded to NGO campaigns for responsible sourcing by pressing and some- times assisting their suppliers to upgrade standards and achieve sustainable forestry certiﬁcation. A second such mechanism is horizontal diffusion within industry associa- tions. In some cases, industry associations have accepted FSC standards and promoted certiﬁcation among their members. In others, they have established alternative business- dominated schemes with weaker initial standards and veriﬁcation requirements. Either way, industry associations have proved important institutional devices for recruiting forestry ﬁrms into certiﬁcation schemes, coordinating their responses to changing demands from external actors, and pooling learning from implementation experience. A second pathway toward a transnational experimentalist regime involves unilateral regulatory initiatives subject to procedural requirements imposed by multilateral insti- tutions like the World Trade Organization (WTO). Thus a large jurisdiction such as the EU or the US may unilaterally seek to extend its internal regulations to transnational supply chains as a condition of market access.WTO rules permit member states to restrict imports in order to protect public health and the environment. But as interpreted by the WTO Appellate Body in its landmark Shrimp-Turtle decisions (WTO 1998, 2001), they Assembling an experimentalist regime C. Overdevest and J. Zeitlin © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 7 Page 8: also require states wishing to restrict imports on these grounds to ensure that their proposed measures are non-discriminatory and proportional to the intended goals, take account of relevant international standards, and consult with their trading partners to minimize the impact on affected third parties (Weinstein & Charnovitz 2001; Scott 2007). These disciplines, when they permit such extensions at all, can thus provide a reﬂexive mechanism for transforming unilateral regulatory initiatives by developed jurisdictions into a joint governance system with stakeholders from the developing world, if not a fully multilateral experimentalist regime. The EU’s FLEGT initiative offers a clear illustration of this pathway. As we will see in Section 4, FLEGT seeks to control exports of illegally logged wood by negotiating Vol- untary Partnership Agreements (VPAs) with developing countries to create export licens- ing systems, based on jointly deﬁned legal standards, regular monitoring and performance review, and third-party veriﬁcation. Local civil society stakeholders partici- pate both in the deﬁnition of “legally harvested wood”and in monitoring its certiﬁcation, each of which is explicitly conceived as revisable in light of the other, while the EU provides development assistance to build up the regulatory capacity of both public and private actors. But the effectiveness of this experimentalist initiative depends on indi- vidual developing countries’ willingness to sign such agreements. To reinforce FLEGT’s effectiveness and extend its geographical scope, the EU has therefore enacted legislation requiring all businesses placing wood products on the European market from whatever source to demonstrate “due diligence” in ensuring that they had not been illegally har- vested. The EU’s approach to combating illegal logging appears likely to be accepted as legitimate not only by the WTO but also by developing countries, because it offers them an opportunity to participate in a jointly governed system of legality assurance, while imposing parallel obligations on European timber ﬁrms to exercise due diligence in respecting local legal standards. A third pathway arises where multilateral treaty obligations do not impose procedural constraints on unilateral regulation, but there is transnational pressure for coordination of separate national and/or regional regimes. Under these circumstances, convergence toward an experimentalist regime can emerge via mutual inﬂuence, transmitted through thin links such as the operation of multinational corporations within each other’s terri- tory, or interchange within transnational advocacy networks. In forestry, as we will see in Section 5, the US has recently adopted legislation subjecting trade in illegally harvested wood to criminal prosecution, with harsher penalties for violators who fail to exercise “due care” in acquiring such products, and obligations for importers of timber products to declare their species and place of origin. Although the US Lacey Act lacks many of the experimentalist features of FLEGT and the EU Timber Regulation, civil society activists and public ofﬁcials from both jurisdictions are exploring opportunities for synergy between the two regimes through exchange of information and experiences on the one hand, and joint pressure on their trading partners to adopt similar schemes on the other. A fourth pathway to the development of transnational experimentalist regimes works through benchmarking and public comparison of competing components of regime complexes. Without such processes of public comparison, which obliged each “private” certiﬁcation scheme to justify and where necessary revise its standards to meet the assessment of external actors, the competition between them could easily have degenerated into a race to the bottom, rather than upward harmonization through mutually productive interaction. FLEGT VPAs and the EU Timber Regulation extend and formalize this logic of accountability by providing for public recognition of private certiﬁcation schemes, subject to comparative assessment of their legal standards, monitoring systems, and veriﬁcation arrangements. A weaker form of such public rec- ognition is also implicit in the US Lacey Act, where participation in a bona ﬁde private certiﬁcation scheme may serve as mitigating evidence of “due care.” Both the EU and the US, ﬁnally, are likely to have a similar impact on their major trading partners, such as China, by pressing them to adopt equivalent legality assurance regimes, whether public or private, as a condition of market access. Taken together, we conclude, these four pathways and the mechanisms underlying them appear to be leading to the de facto emergence of a joined-up transnational experimentalist regime for sustainable forestry and control of illegal logging, which blurs and may ultimately efface standard distinctions between public and private regulation. 3. From failed public governance to private experimentalism Transnational efforts to build a regulatory regime for forestry date back to 1992, when environmental groups and northern countries concerned with high rates of tropical deforestation proposed a binding global convention at the UN Conference on Environ- ment and Development. Developing countries led by Malaysia rejected that proposal, fearing that their capacity to achieve economic development would be constrained by northern demands for conservation, which they also viewed as a disguised form of protectionism (Bernstein & Cashore 2004). The Rio Earth Summit instead produced only a set of non-binding forest management principles, which enshrined the principle of national sovereignty over forest exploitation. Over the ensuing 30 years, several additional attempts, including the UN Intergovernmental Panel on Forests (IPF) and its successor, the Intergovernmental Forum on Forests (IFF), created international dialogues on forest sustainability but as at Rio, “the IPF delegates failed to agree on major issues” (Rosendal 2001, p. 450; Humphreys 2006). Efforts by some northern governments to tackle this issue by imposing unilateral environmental standards or mandatory eco-labeling systems for imported timber were likewise blocked by their incompatibility with the rules of the global trade regime. Thus, for example, the Austrian government was obliged to withdraw a law banning import of unsustainably harvested tropical wood products in the face of complaints by develop- ing countries to the WTO’s predecessor, the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT). An earlier effort during the late 1980s to develop a system for certifying eco- logically acceptable forest products through the International Tropical Timber Trade Organization (ITTO) similarly foundered on opposition from timber-exporting coun- tries and charges of GATT-incompatibility (Bartley 2007). 3.1. Experimenting with private certiﬁcation Such failures of multilateral agreements and national public governance, however, can create openings through which non-state actors creatively move parts of a complex issue Assembling an experimentalist regime C. Overdevest and J. Zeitlin © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 9 Page 10: forward. Forestry provides a clear illustration. Thus in response to the failure of nations to agree on common global rules at the 1992 Rio conference, civil society groups began developing private standards and certiﬁcation systems. A year after the Rio debacle, environmental NGOs, businesses, foundations, and social organizations launched the Forest Stewardship Council. The FSC has a number of experimentalist features which explicitly address the impasse at Rio by establishing a deliberative, multi-stakeholder process for setting and revising broad, principles-based standards for sustainable forest management, adapting them to local conditions, certifying their voluntary application by ﬁrms, independently verifying the results, and requiring corrective action where needed. In order to overcome the mistrust and resentments that blocked agreement at Rio, the FSC creatively balanced the inﬂuence of environmental, business, and social organiza- tions, as well as southern and northern interests, in its central standard setting and revision body. Standards and procedures are determined through deliberation and super- majority voting by three equal chambers representing environmental, economic, and social interests, with equal weight within them for members from the global north and the global south. The FSC’s principles include respect for labor and indigenous people’s rights, as well as biodiversity, ecological sustainability, and environmental management requirements. These general principles are elaborated through more speciﬁc global standards and criteria, which are in turn adapted to local conditions by national or regional chapters. Compliance with FSC principles and standards is veriﬁed by independently accred- ited third-party monitoring organizations. Conformance audits occur every ﬁve years, supplemented by annual surveillance audits. Audit teams review forest management planning documentation, ﬁrm ﬁnancial data, and contracts for services (such as chemical applications), as well as conduct consultations with forest employees and other local stakeholders, such as NGOs, community leaders, resource managers, and neighbors about problems with the certiﬁed forest’s management. FSC audits also enable continuous learning at the forest management unit (FMU) level. Principle 8 states that certiﬁed management units must continually assess the condition of the forest, monitoring harvest yields, growth rates, compositional changes in ﬂora and fauna, environmental and social impacts of harvesting, and costs, productivity, and efﬁciency of forest management. Results of such monitoring must be incorporated into the revision of management plans. In theory, therefore, the FSC establishes a process of continuous learning, whereby ﬁrms should be able to assess regularly updated infor- mation on environmental impacts, growth rates, yields, and so on. This also means that auditors have access to information about the relative effectiveness of forest management practices, which they could use to develop performance-based comparisons across cer- tiﬁed units. Such comparisons could be used to put additional pressure on laggards and leaders and to inform the regular three-year General Assembly Meeting of the FSC, where the chambers vote on any changes needed in the standards. To date, however, the FSC has inadequately developed its own capacity for experimentalist “learning by monitoring” (Sabel 1994), even if the institutional preconditions for the functioning of such a system are already in place, because it fails to pool, compare, and thereby induce greater reﬂec- tion on local standards implementation. To promote accountability of both certiﬁed forests and monitors, the FSC requires accredited auditors to publish public summaries of the audit reports on their websites. Descriptions of the forest are recorded (location, management objectives, size of holding, Assembling an experimentalist regime C. Overdevest and J. Zeitlin © 2012 Blackwell Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 10 Page 11: types of sites) and documentation of the audit ﬁndings are provided, including major and minor non-conformances, summaries of ﬁeld and ofﬁce assessments, and stakeholder interviews. These audit summaries have enabled watchdog groups and activists to monitor the functioning of the FSC and thereby contribute to securing the FSC’s accountability to the broader forest governance community. The FSC also certiﬁes supply chains. In order for retail products to carry an FSC label, each step along the supply chains must be certiﬁed. Traditionally, chain of custody audits have been built on “paper-based” systems, where checkers evaluate whether companies have systems in place to track FSC certiﬁed wood through the supply chain. Recent advances, however, suggest that DNA ﬁngerprinting technologies may soon enable genetically based spot checks to supplement paper and system audits (Auld et al. 2010). The FSC thus displays several key features of an experimentalist governance archi- tecture. Its global organization establishes broad framework goals for “sustainable forests.” The national and regional chapters are given discretion to customize these goals to local conditions. Individual FMUs apply these standards and report regularly on their performance through audits. Theoretically, the FSC can orchestrate a process of infor- mation pooling and review in which the results of local experimentation with sustainable forestry are compared with those of others employing different means to the same ends. Auditors do in fact require that local units show that they are taking appropriate cor- rective measures, although the FSC could do more to require continuous improvement from experience-based learning across, as well as within, FMUs, by endogenizing lessons learned from implementing the standards in regular revisions at General Assembly meetings. 3.2. The emergence of private certiﬁcation schemes could have led to a highly fragmented regime in which high-standards forest operations joined the FSC for strategic advantage while others looked to weaker schemes to shield themselves from public regulation (Bartley 2007). In fact, as competing industry certiﬁcation schemes emerged, progressive ﬁrms and those under strong state regulatory standards did join the FSC for strategic advantage, while many large forest industry companies and small forest landowners joined weaker competing schemes, creating an apparently fragmented governance space (Cashore et al. 2004). However, three key mechanisms have combined to encourage more positive interac- tions between the FSC and its competitors (Overdevest 2004). Rather than fragmenta- tion, competition among private schemes instead resulted in mutual adjustment, learning from experience, and increased accountability of schemes to one another and to external audiences. This accountability, initiated by downstream customers, government procure- ment ofﬁces, and NGOs through “benchmarking for equivalence,” enhanced the social nature of their rationality, explaining how a reﬂexive competition emerged which yielded productive interactions rather than a race to the bottom. These regime dynamics thus call attention to how issue areas characterized by stra- tegic uncertainty and complex interdependence, in which actors like forest companies cannot achieve narrow self-interested goals because they depend on the approval of others (such as consumers, retailers, regulators), may generate conditions for a more other-regarding rationality to emerge. One mechanism through which private forest certiﬁcation has expanded and devel- oped as an alternative to the weak international regime created by states following Rio is vertical diffusion along global supply chains from downstream customers to upstream producers. Retailers, branded manufacturers, and public procurement agencies have responded to NGO campaigns for sustainable sourcing by pressing and sometimes assist- ing their suppliers to upgrade standards and achieve forestry certiﬁcation. Denmark, Belgium, France, Germany, Netherlands, the UK, Japan, and New Zealand all accept private certiﬁcation as evidence of legality and/or sustainability in meeting green public procurement standards (Gulbrandsen 2011). In combination, public and private supply chains have thus proved an important conduit for partial and selective transnational forest regulation in the absence of a multilateral regime. A second mechanism through which private certiﬁcation has expanded and devel- oped as an alternative to the weak public international forestry regime is horizontal diffusion within industry associations.
